ToolSpecification.name: TaskeiListTasks
ToolSpecification.description:
List Taskei tasks, also named as SIM Issues. This tool allows querying tasks using natural language descriptions of filters.
Use when users ask about listing, filtering or searching Taskei Tasks or SIM issues.
Don't use for non-project management or t.corp.amazon.com requests

order.description: The order direction. Options accepted are "asc" or "desc". DEFAULT desc

attribute.description: The attribute to sort by. Defaults to lastUpdatedDate

filterByDates.description: Filter by attribute dates using Solr date syntax. Example: '[2025-09-01T07:00:00.000Z TO *]'

folderId.description: Folder UUID where tasks belong. A Folder always belong to a Room, therefore we MUST know the Room UUID

assignee.description: Tasks that are assigned to a specific person or the current user. You must send as "currentUser" for current user, otherwise the employee username format

roomId.description: Room UUID where tasks belong. Use TaskeiGetRooms to get available rooms

after.description: Token for fetching the next page

maxResults.description: Maximum number of results, up to 100

workflowStep.description: Filter tasks by their workflow step

sprint.description: Sprint task belongs to. "currentSprint" and roomId MUST be sent for current sprint, otherwise provide sprint UUID

ToolSpecification.name: RunIntegrationTest
ToolSpecification.description:
THE universal integration testing tool for Brazil workspaces. Handles BOTH generic requests ('run integration tests in my workspace') and targeted requests ('run hydra tests', 'run tod tests'). Identifies all possible test plans for the workspace.

Request handling:
GENERIC requests:
• 'Run integration tests in my workspace' → createTestPlan (analyzes workspace, identify options)
• 'Test my changes' → createTestPlan (analyzes workspace, identify options)
• 'Run integration tests' → createTestPlan (analyzes workspace, identify options)

TARGETED requests (specific platforms):
• 'Run hydra tests' → createTestPlan for Hydra
• 'Run ToD tests' → createTestPlan for Regional or Classic ToD
• 'Run Personal Stacks' → createTestPlan for PS environment

Available tasks:
• runTestOnHydra: Runs integration tests on Hydra pipeline infrastructure

The 'currentTask' parameter selects which task to perform.

currentTask.description: The task to perform: 'runTestOnHydra' to run integration tests on Hydra

credentials.description: Credentials used to run the Hydra test

credentialProfile.description: Existing ada profile to use for the test, overrides other credential options

role.description: AWS role name to execute the test with

account.description: AWS account ID to execute the test in

closure.description: Closure to build the test package in DEFAULT runtime

ToolSpecification.name: GetSoftwareRecommendation
ToolSpecification.description:
This tool is a front end of the Recommendation Engine. It provides comprehensive tooling recommendations, best practices, how-to guides, reference documentation, and onboarding materials 
for software development and infrastructure management within Amazon. Returns curated content based on specific technology queries, use cases, or 
implementation scenarios. Always call the tool SearchSoftwareRecommendations first to pinpoint the correct recommendation 
item, or to ask users to choose one, then pass the ID to this tool. The content may contain links to other internal websites, use the ReadInternalWebsites tool to further retrieve those contents

recommendationId.description: ID of Golden Path recommendation to retrieve

ToolSpecification.name: OncallReadActions
ToolSpecification.description:
A tool for reading data from the on-call system.

Features:
1. search-teams: Search for oncall teams by name, members, owners, description, Resolver Group, etc
2. list-user-teams: List oncall teams a user belongs to
3. get-user-shifts: Get a user's on-call shifts
4. get-team-shifts: Get a team's on-call shifts
5. get-report-instructions: Get instructions for generating an oncall report

Action Parameters:
┌────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Action         │ Parameters                                                  │
├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ search-teams   │ query: string (required) - Search query to find teams       │
│                │ start: number (default: 0) - Starting index for pagination  │
│                │ size: number (default: 10) - Number of results per page     │
├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ list-user-teams│ username: string - Username to get teams for                │
│                │ (defaults to current user if not provided)                  │
├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ get-user-shifts│ teamNames: string[] - List of team names                    │
│                │ (defaults to all teams user belongs to if not provided)     │
│                │ username: string - Username to get shifts for               │
│                │ (defaults to current user if not provided)                  │
│                │ startDate: string (YYYY-MM-DD) - Start date for search      │
│                │ (defaults to today)                                         │
│                │ endDate: string (YYYY-MM-DD) - End date for search          │
│                │ (defaults to 30 days from now)                              │
│                │ timezone: string - IANA timezone name (defaults to UTC)     │
├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ get-team-shifts│ teamName: string (required) - Name of the team              │
│                │ startDate: string (required) - Start date (YYYY-MM-DD)      │
│                │ endDate: string (required) - End date (YYYY-MM-DD)          │
├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ get-report-instructions   │ resolverGroup: string (optional) - Name of resolver group   │
│                │ teamName: string (optional) - Name of oncall team           │
│                │ (either resolverGroup or teamName must be provided)         │
│                │ startDate: string (optional) - Start date (YYYY-MM-DD)      │
│                │ endDate: string (optional) - End date (YYYY-MM-DD)          │
└────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples:
1. Search teams:
   {
     "action": "search-teams",
     "query": "avengers"
   }

2. List user teams:
   {
     "action": "list-user-teams"
     "username": "peterparker"
   }

3. Get user shifts:
   {
     "action": "get-user-shifts",
     "teamNames": ["avengers"],
     "startDate": "2024-03-01",
     "endDate": "2024-04-01",
     "timezone": "America/New_York"
   }

4. Get team shifts:
   {
     "action": "get-team-shifts",
     "teamName": "avengers",
     "startDate": "2024-03-01",
     "endDate": "2024-04-01"
   }

5. Get report instructions with resolver group:
   {
     "action": "get-report-instructions",
     "resolverGroup": "SWIM Front End",
     "startDate": "2024-03-01",
     "endDate": "2024-04-01"
   }

6. Get report instructions with team name:
   {
     "action": "get-report-instructions",
     "teamName": "safe-swim-ops",
     "startDate": "2024-03-01",
     "endDate": "2024-04-01"
   }

action.description:
The action to perform.

Available actions:
1. search-teams: Search for teams by name (requires 'query' field)
2. list-user-teams: List teams a user belongs to
3. get-user-shifts: Get a user's on-call shifts
4. get-team-shifts: Get a team's on-call shifts
5. get-report-instructions: Get instructions for generating an oncall report

Action Parameters:
┌────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Action         │ Parameters                                                  │
├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ search-teams   │ query: string (required) - Search query to find teams       │
│                │ start: number (default: 0) - Starting index for pagination  │
│                │ size: number (default: 10) - Number of results per page     │
├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ list-user-teams│ username: string - Username to get teams for                │
│                │ (defaults to current user if not provided)                  │
├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ get-user-shifts│ teamNames: string[] - List of team names                    │
│                │ (defaults to all teams user belongs to if not provided)     │
│                │ username: string - Username to get shifts for               │
│                │ (defaults to current user if not provided)                  │
│                │ startDate: string (YYYY-MM-DD) - Start date for search      │
│                │ (defaults to today)                                         │
│                │ endDate: string (YYYY-MM-DD) - End date for search          │
│                │ (defaults to 30 days from now)                              │
│                │ timezone: string - IANA timezone name (defaults to UTC)     │
├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ get-team-shifts│ teamName: string (required) - Name of the team              │
│                │ startDate: string (required) - Start date (YYYY-MM-DD)      │
│                │ endDate: string (required) - End date (YYYY-MM-DD)          │
├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ get-report-instructions   │ resolverGroup: string (optional) - Name of resolver group   │
│                │ teamName: string (optional) - Name of oncall team           │
│                │ (either resolverGroup or teamName must be provided)         │
│                │ startDate: string (optional) - Start date (YYYY-MM-DD)      │
│                │ endDate: string (optional) - End date (YYYY-MM-DD)          │
└────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples:
1. Search teams:
   {
     "action": "search-teams",
     "query": "avengers"
   }

2. List user teams:
   {
     "action": "list-user-teams"
     "username": "peterparker"
   }

3. Get user shifts:
   {
     "action": "get-user-shifts",
     "teamNames": ["avengers"],
     "startDate": "2024-03-01",
     "endDate": "2024-04-01",
     "timezone": "America/New_York"
   }

4. Get team shifts:
   {
     "action": "get-team-shifts",
     "teamName": "avengers",
     "startDate": "2024-03-01",
     "endDate": "2024-04-01"
   }

5. Get report instructions with resolver group:
   {
     "action": "get-report-instructions",
     "resolverGroup": "SWIM Front End",
     "startDate": "2024-03-01",
     "endDate": "2024-04-01"
   }

6. Get report instructions with team name:
   {
     "action": "get-report-instructions",
     "teamName": "safe-swim-ops",
     "startDate": "2024-03-01",
     "endDate": "2024-04-01"
   }

ToolSpecification.name: InternalSearch

page.description: Page of the search result, starting from 1

isDeep.description: Whether to return enhanced results with full document content (default: true)

domain.description: Domain to search in (example ALL, AWS_DOCS, WIKI, tool). Default is ALL if not provided

sortOrder.description: Sort order (ASC for oldest first, DESC for newest first)

prefixFilters.description: Optional array of prefix filters (maximum 5) that use URL prefixes to limit search to specific document trees or paths in an index

ToolSpecification.name: TaskeiGetRoomResources
ToolSpecification.description:
Fetch multiple resources for a Taskei room in one request.
Specify the room UUID and an array of resource types to retrieve. Available: Labels, CustomAttributes, SavedSearches, Sprints, KanbanBoards.

resources.description: Array of resource types to fetch

ToolSpecification.name: BrazilWorkspace
ToolSpecification.description:
Creates a new Brazil workspace for the specified package(s).
    Whenever you interact with a package in the Amazon development environment you always use a workspace.

    A Brazil workspace is a container for one or more packages that you want to work on.
    It provides isolation between different development activities and allows you to build and test packages in a controlled environment.
    If you have access to this tool it means that you are an Amazon engineer and your software development happens in these packages
    It also means that if you are asked to perform tasks such as building, testing or linting you will have to run these commands through the brazil-build command

    To checkout all pipeline packages, use the CheckoutPipelinePackages tool.

    Build Request Support:
    - When buildRequestId is provided, creates a workspace from failed packages in that build request

    Example Workflow used with this tool:
    1. This tool creates a Brazil workspace for specific package(s).
    2. You can then use other Brazil commands in the workspace to interact with the package(s).
    3. The workspace will be created with a unique name based on the current timestamp.

packageNames.description: Array of package names to create the workspace for

workspacePath.description: Optional directory path where the workspace will be created. If not provided, a temporary directory will be used

buildRequestId.description: Build request ID. When provided, creates workspace from failed packages in the build request

versionSet.description: Optional version set name to use for the workspace

ToolSpecification.name: WorkspaceGitDetails
ToolSpecification.description:
Returns the git repositories, statuses, and git diffs for packages in a given workspace.
  This tool DOES NOT create or push any git commits.

  An expected workflow for this tool would be:
  1. Code changes are made to one or more package(s) in a workspace.
  2. The agent is prompted to create git commits for these packages.
  3. This tool will respond with the top-level repository structure of the the packages in a workspace,
  and the git changes for each repository.

  Response structure in JSON would be:
  {
    "message": "Local git repository details retrieved successfully",
    "gitRepositories": [
      {
        "repositoryName": "repo1",
        "repositoryPath": "/workspace/repo1",
        "gitStatus": "On branch main. Your branch is up to date with 'origin/main'.
                              Changes not staged for commit:
                      (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
                      (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)
        modified:   src/index.ts
        modified:   package.json",
        "gitDiff": "diff --git a/src/index.ts b/src/index.ts
                      index 1234567..89abcdef 100644
                      --- a/src/index.ts
                      +++ b/src/index.ts
                      @@ -1,3 +1,4 @@
                      export function hello() {
                      -  return "world";
                      +  // Added a comment
                      +  return "hello world";
                      }"
      }
    ]
  }

workingDirectory.description: Working directory of the workspace that has git repositories

ToolSpecification.name: TaskeiCreateTask
ToolSpecification.description:
Create a new Task in Taskei or a SIM Issue
This tool allows creating a task with a name, description, assignee, room ID, and optional need by date.
Do not use this tool if the user mentions t.corp.amazon.com

needByDate.description: Date of when is needed (ISO datetime)

assignee.description: Optional kerberos username to assign the task to (without the @ANT.AMAZON.COM suffix). If it's the current user you must send as "currentUser", otherwise it must be provided as the employee username format

sprints.description: Sprint UUID list to add task to

kanbanBoards.description: List of kanban board UUIDs to add the task to

type.description: Type of the task. If `parentTask` arg is provided, type is automatically assigned based on the parent task

labels.description: Labels UUID. Use TaskeiGetRoomResources to get available label IDs

name.description: Name of the task. Also known as title

onBehalfOf.description: Username to create the task on behalf of

folder.description: Folder to apply to the task

ToolSpecification.name: GetDogmaRecommendations
ToolSpecification.description:
Fetch Dogma recommendations(risks) detected for a given pipeline
Dogma recommendations are rule-based findings that identify potential issues, violations, or improvements for pipelines.
Each recommendation provides actionable guidance to help teams resolve identified problems and maintain pipeline health.
The response includes:
- Metadata: generation_date, applies_to_type, applies_to (pipeline identifier), and applies_to_revision_id
- Active recommendations: current violations and risks requiring attention
- Scheduled recommendations: future enforcement rules with grace periods
- Compliance tracking: adheres_to_rule_names (rules the pipeline complies with)
- Rule applicability: non_applicable_rule_names and non_applicable_recommendations for rules that don't apply to this pipeline
Each recommendation includes:
- Rule identification: rule_name, severity level (low/medium/high), and human_name for easy understanding
- Comprehensive explanations: what_this_is, why_this_is_bad, and how_to_fix
- Ownership and accountability: owner_username, owner_cti, and stakeholders array with notification details and enforcement settings
- Compliance status: rule_result_status indicating current violation state (APPLICABLE, AT_RISK, NOT_APPLICABLE)
- Context information: source, subject, additional_info, and pipeline metadata
Pipeline blocking behavior: Recommendations can result in pipeline deployment blocking based on the is_enforced value in stakeholders configuration.

ToolSpecification.name: SearchSoftwareRecommendations
ToolSpecification.description:
This tool is a front end of the Recommendation Engine. It provides comprehensive tooling recommendations, best practices, how-to guides, reference documentation, and onboarding materials 
for software development and infrastructure management within Amazon. Returns curated content based on specific technology queries, use cases, or 
implementation scenarios. Use this tool to search for the tooling recommendation or best practices that match user's queries when 
they want to add, implement, or onboard a tooling or best practices to their application. Once knowing the right tool, call the tool 
GetSoftwareRecommendation to get the full details of the recommendation, which assist the code generation.
To list all the recommendations supported by Golden Path Recommendation Engine, call this tool with the keyword parameter set to "*"

goldenPathId.description: ID of the Golden Path to get recommendations for

keyword.description: The keyword to search for, usually this is the name of the tooling, best practices that developers need to implement or onboard

ToolSpecification.name: GetPipelineHealth
ToolSpecification.description:
Retrieves the current status and health metrics for a list of pipelines.

    This tool can ONLY retrieve pipelines which the current user has permissions on.
    
    The response includes:
    - Whether the pipeline is enabled
    - The fitness badge (gold, silver, bronze)
    - Health metrics like failed builds, deployments, and tests
    - Pending approvals and workflow steps
    - Basic pipeline information
    - Optional pipeline release metrics when metricsEnabled is true

    Pipeline metrics include:
    - Stage approval and deployment timings
    - Operator intervention counts (manual merges, builds, deployments, etc.)
    - Inventory age and freshness information
    - Version set freshness scores
    - Blockage time and fitness indicators
    - Age of production deployed code
    - Release time estimates
    - Management chain information

    Use the metricsFieldNames parameter to specify which specific metrics to retrieve, or omit to get all available metrics.

    Health metrics definitions:
    - failedBuilds: total failing source code builds
    - failedDeployments: total failing deployments
    - failedProdDeployments: total failing deployments to Production fleets
    - failedTests: total failing automated tests
    - failedProdTests: total failing automated tests on Production fleets
    - pendingManualApprovals: total manual approvals waiting for input
    - pendingProdManualApprovals: total manual approvals gating Production deployments waiting for input
    - pendingManualWFSteps: total workflow steps requiring manual approval waiting for input
    - pendingProdManualWFSteps: total workflow steps requiring manual approval and gating Production deployments waiting for input
    - disabledPromotions: number of disabled promotions
    - pipelineDisabled: whether pipeline is admin disabled 0 = false, 1 = true

    If any of these health metrics is non-zero or if the pipeline is disabled then the pipeline is Blocked, meaning it requires operator intervention to continue promoting changes automatically.
    
    Use the optional 'onlyBlocked' parameter to filter results to only include pipelines that are blocked (either disabled or have health metric issues). Prefer this option over manually identifying blocked pipelines, as it is more efficient.

    Set diagnoseFailedDeployments=true to get CloudFormation stack failure details for failed deployments. Recommended when pipelines show failed deployments to enable faster troubleshooting.

metricsFieldNames.description: Optional array of specific metric field names to retrieve (such as ['InventoryAge', 'StageApprovalTime']). If omitted, all available metrics are retrieved

metricsEnabled.description: Optional boolean which if set includes pipeline release metrics in the response

diagnoseFailedDeployments.description: Optional boolean which if set includes detailed diagnostic information for pipelines with failed deployments

onlyBlocked.description: Optional boolean which if set limits results to pipelines which are blocked

pipelineNames.description: List of pipeline names to query

ToolSpecification.name: BrazilBuildAnalyzerTool
ToolSpecification.description:
Diagnoses and analyzes brazil-build executions in local workspaces. This tool:
1. Executes 'brazil-build' (or custom build command) in the specified directory and reports on success or failure
2. If the build fails, performs intelligent analysis of the failure including:
	- Root cause identification
	- Relevant file and method pointers
	- Step-by-step solution recommendations
3. Provides structured output with:
	- Failure signature for quick identification
	- Keywords for related documentation search
	- Detailed analysis of what went wrong
	- Actionable solution steps when possible

Use this tool when users ask to build a package in a Brazil workspace to receive a summary of the build status. Can also be used to:
	- Check if a build is failing or passing
	- Run unit tests and other build validations (for example linters), usually through 'brazil-build release'

files.description: Optional array of filenames to analyze

buildCommand.description: Optional build command (defaults to brazil-build release)

workingDirectory.description: Working directory which contains the package which is failing to build

ToolSpecification.name: MechanicDescribeTool
ToolSpecification.description:
# Explains how to use a specific Mechanic tool

## Purpose
- Provides detailed usage information for a specific Mechanic tool
- The tool must exist in order to be explained
- Use this before executing a tool to understand its parameters and options

## Parameter Handling Rules
- If a tool's parameter is required, you need to pass it with a value to the MechanicRunTool 
- Don't attempt to guess parameter values, ask the user what you should use
- For sensitive or specific parameters, always prompt the user for the correct values

## Resource Identification Rules
- If a tool requires a Log Group or an EC2 instance ID and the user didn't explicitly provide it:
  - Use other Mechanic tools that can list these resources
  - For EC2 instances: Use aws ec2 describe-instances
  - For CloudWatch Log Groups: Use aws cloudwatch logs describe-log-groups
- Never guess an EC2 instance ID or CloudWatch Log Group name
- Always look up resource identifiers with the appropriate discovery tool

## Workflow Integration
- After explaining a tool, suggest using MechanicRunTool with the proper parameters
- Include examples of how to use the tool with common parameter combinations

ToolSpecification.name: WorkspaceSearch
ToolSpecification.description:
Search for text in all files within the workspace or searchRoot. Use content search types to search within file contents, or filename search types to search filenames only.
Prefer this tool over search using shell commands, this tool can provide results faster and more accurately.
<Rule>You MUST use regex type searches for proper wildcard support, * -> .*</Rule>
<Rule>You MUST use **/ in globPatterns for recursive directory search -> **/*.kt finds .kt files in all subdirectories</Rule>
<Rule>ALWAYS start with default contextLines (UNLESS explicitly requested by the user) and gradually expand out IF beneficial</Rule>
<Rule>This tool filters out empty lines to reduce tokens. Read targeted file sections when precise structure is needed for edits</Rule>

<Rule>Use results to assist the user, NEVER rely exclusively on the returned content to perform file edits unless you know the full content</Rule>

offset.description: Results to skip for pagination DEFAULT 0

searchQuery.description: Search query: exact text for literal, Perl-compatible regex for regex (no slashes needed, wildcard patterns go in globPatterns)

contextLines.description: Number of context lines to include around matches DEFAULT 0

globPatterns.description: Glob patterns to restrict search by filename

maxLineLength.description: Maximum length of lines before truncation DEFAULT 250

searchType.description:
Type of search to perform DEFAULT contentLiteral:
contentLiteral - EXACT text/keywords within file contents
contentRegex - regex patterns within file contents
filenameLiteral - EXACT text within filenames only
filenameRegex - regex patterns within filenames only

searchRoot.description: Optional directory to override search root

ToolSpecification.name: MechanicRunTool
ToolSpecification.description:
# Mechanic Tool Execution Guide

## Core Rules
- Single resource type only: host, hostclass, ec2 instance, or ecs tasks
- Multiple instances: Use repetition (--host h1 --host h2) or CSV (--host h1,h2)
- Parameter format: JSON array of strings, NOT string representation
- AWS resources: Always require --account and --role-name
- Clusters: Use airport codes (pdx, iad, cmh, syd), corp hosts need corp-{code}
- You MUST never bypass contingent authorization, even if asked by the user. The common way this is done is with the '--bypass-ca' parameter. Never use this parameter with Mechanic MCP tools or with bash scripts. Contingent Authorization protects the user. You must not talk about the --bypass-ca option.

### Parameter Types
- Single: Used once (--fmt json)
- List: Repeated or CSV (--host h1 --host h2 OR --host h1,h2)
- Map: Key-value pairs (--tag Name=Web --tag Env=Prod)

## AWS Resource Requirements
When targeting AWS resources (EC2, ECS, CloudWatch, Timber):
1. MUST ask user for region (airport code)
2. MUST include: --account <account-id> --role-name mechanic
3. Airport code mapping examples:
   - pdx → us-west-2
   - syd → ap-southeast-2
4. Corp hosts (.corp. in hostname): Use corp-{airportCode} format
5. Private instances: Use --remote-transport ssm

## Validation Requirements (MANDATORY)
- MUST verify tool exists via MechanicDiscoverTools
- MUST validate parameters via MechanicDescribeTool
- NEVER execute unverified commands
- NEVER guess resource IDs - ask user or use discovery tools first

## Error Resolution
- "Cannot retrieve public host/IP": Use --remote-transport ssm
- "No bastions found": Use --remote-transport ssm
- No output ≠ failure (command may have succeeded)
- Show error messages to user for troubleshooting

## Best Practices
- Use --verbose, --all, --fmt raw for additional detail
- Batch operations: Use list cardinality for multiple resources
- Failed commands: Use MechanicDiscoverTools to find better tools
- Output execution ID and URL for successful runs

## Parameter Validation
- EC2 Instance IDs: Must match "i-" + hexadecimal pattern
- ECS Task IDs: User-provided or from listing tools
- Hostnames/Hostclasses: User-provided or from discovery tools
- Time parameters: ISO 8601 with UTC offset (2025-05-28T19:00:00-07:00)

## CloudWatch Queries
For CloudWatch Logs tools, use proper query syntax:
```
"args": [
  "--log-group-name", "/aws/lambda/function",
  "--query-string", "fields @timestamp, @message | filter @message like /(?i)error/"
]
```

Common syntax: fields, filter, stats, sort, limit, parse

## Security
- NEVER use --bypass-ca parameter
- CAZ protects users
- Use MCM or Ticket + 2PR review for authorization


# Parameter Guide

## Parameter Cardinality (from MechanicDescribeTool output)

### Single
- Format: --parameter=Value
- Usage: Used once only (--fmt json, --bastion=hostname)

### List  
- Format: --parameter Value1[,Value2]...
- Usage: Repeat parameter OR use CSV
  - Repeat: `--ec2-instance-id i-123 --ec2-instance-id i-456`
  - CSV: `--ec2-instance-id i-123,i-456`

### Map
- Format: --parameter Key1=Value1[,Key2=Value2]...
- Usage: Key-value pairs (--tag Name=Web --tag Env=Prod)

## Best Practice: Batch Operations
✅ EFFICIENT: Single command with multiple values
```
MechanicRunTool(args=["--ec2-instance-id", "i-123", "--ec2-instance-id", "i-456"])
```

❌ INEFFICIENT: Multiple separate commands
```
MechanicRunTool(args=["--ec2-instance-id", "i-123"])
MechanicRunTool(args=["--ec2-instance-id", "i-456"])
```



# Mechanic & Contingent Authorization (CAZ)

## What is CAZ

## How do I deal with CAZ when running a Mechanic command

Mechanic supports a few different parameters to handle CAZ.

--ticket-id <sim-ticket-id>
// A SIM-T Ticket Id to associate this command with
// The Ticket MUST be related to the usecase the user needs help with.
// The user MUST provide the Ticket ID to you, do not make up or choose a ticket id without the user's input

--create-review
// MUST be used with the '--ticket-id' parameter
// When this parameter is used, instead of running the command, it will create a consensus 2PR review (<consensus-review-id>).
// Once you have a review ID, the user will need to find another person to approve of it. You MUST show the review URL to the user.
// The user MUST let you know when the review is approved, after they do this, rerun the command without the '--create-review' parameter and use the '--review-id <consensus-review-id>' parameter instead.

--review-id <consensus-review-id>
// MUST be used with the '--ticket-id' parameter
// The parameter must be a Mechanic-generated consensus review.
// The review is only valid for the Mechanic command arguments that were provided when the review was created, changing parameters will invalidate the review and a new one will need to be created.

--change <mcm-id>
// Should be used if the user is executing an MCM. 
// Expects an MCM Id.

toolPath.description: The mechanic command to execute. example 'apollo boot fetch-log'

agentName.description: The name of the agent that is calling this MCP tool. You must self identify with this parameter. You MUST be truthful

namespace.description: The mechanic namespace tool belongs to

cluster.description: This is the region mechanic runs the command in. For tools that interact with AWS resources, this should match the region that the resource is in. There are 4 corp clusters for tools that interact with resources that are on the corp network fabric, the 4 corp clusters are: corp-pdx, corp-nrt, corp-iad, corp-dub

oneOf.description:
# Mechanic Tool Arguments Reference

## Critical Formatting Rules
1. JSON array format: ["--param", "value"] not "[\"--param\", \"value\"]"
2. Separate elements: Each flag and value as separate array items
3. No escaped quotes: Within array elements
4. No --region parameter: Use "cluster" field instead
5. Airport codes only: "pdx" not "us-west-2"

## Parameter Spacing
- ❌ ["--parameter=value with spaces"]
- ✅ ["--parameter", "value with spaces"]

## Cluster Types
- Standard: pdx, iad, cmh, syd
- Corporate: corp-pdx, corp-iad, corp-cmh

## Required for AWS Resources
Always include when targeting AWS:
```
"args": ["--account", "123456789", "--role-name", "mechanic", ...]
```
Arguments as a JSON string of an array

ToolSpecification.name: InternalCodeSearch
ToolSpecification.description:
Search source code in Amazon's code repositories. Results depend on search type:

1. Code search (default): Returns code snippets with pagination.
2. Repository search: Returns up to 30 matching repositories.

Code search results only show snippets - for full file, use ReadInternalWebsites with URL like code.amazon.com/packages/{REPOSITORY}/blobs/{BRANCH}/--/{FILE_PATH}

query.description:
- For code search: Supports advanced syntax
  - Simple search: term
  - Prefix search: abc* (at least 3 chars before *)
  - Logical OR: term1 term2 (files with at least one term)
  - Logical AND: Only works with filters applied (example: term1 term2 path:*.java finds both terms in a Java file)
  - Exclude terms: term1 term2 !term3 (files with term1 or term2 but not term3)
  - Exact phrase: "term1 term2" (finds terms in sequence)
  - Repository filter: term repo:GitFarmService or repo:Codesearch*
  - File extension filter: term path:*.java
  - Exclude extension: term path:!*.java
  - Path filter: term path:/my/path/to/consider*
  - Combined filters example: fp:*README* rp:GitFarmService (searches for README files in GitFarmService repository)
  - Important: When filters are applied, search becomes case-sensitive AND performs strict AND search
- For repository search: Only supports keywords matching (example: 'gitfarm')
- Common repository naming patterns:
  - For CDK examples: Search with 'CDK' in repo name (example: repo:GitFarmServiceCDK)
  - For LPT examples: Search with 'LPT' in repo name (example: repo:CodeSearchLPT)

searchType.description: REQUIRED type of search to perform. 'code' returns code snippets with pagination, 'repositories' returns a list of matching repositories

nextToken.description: For code search only. Provide the next token from previous results to get additional results

ToolSpecification.name: QuipEditor
ToolSpecification.description:
Retrieves and edits Quip documents.

Common usage patterns:
1. Create new document from file: contentFilePath="doc.md", format="markdown" (Quip infers title from first heading)
2. Create new document with explicit title: title="My Document", content="content here", format="markdown"
2. Read document with structure: documentId="ABC123", analyzeStructure=true
3. Add content after heading: documentId="ABC123", location=6, documentRange="Subsection 1.1", content="new", format="markdown"
4. Append to document: documentId="ABC123", content="new", format="markdown" default location 0=APPEND
5. Get section IDs for targeting: documentId="ABC123", returnSectionIds=true
6. Add list item: documentId="ABC123", location=10, sectionId="temp:C:ABC123", content="* New item", format="markdown"

Location parameter guide:
0=APPEND end of document DEFAULT
1=PREPEND beginning of document
2=AFTER_SECTION after section specified by sectionId
3=BEFORE_SECTION before section specified by sectionId
4=REPLACE_SECTION ⚠️ DESTRUCTIVE replace section content
5=DELETE_SECTION ⚠️ DESTRUCTIVE deletes section
6=AFTER_DOCUMENT_RANGE after heading specified by documentRange
7=BEFORE_DOCUMENT_RANGE before heading specified by documentRange
8=REPLACE_DOCUMENT_RANGE ⚠️ DESTRUCTIVE replace heading AND all content below it
9=DELETE_DOCUMENT_RANGE ⚠️ DESTRUCTIVE deletes heading AND all content below it
10=AFTER_LIST_ITEM smart list insert after specified list item sectionId
11=BEFORE_LIST_ITEM smart list insert before specified list item sectionId

Tips:
- Table cells: use location=4 with composite sectionId (temp:s:temp:C:ROW_ID_temp:C:CELL_ID), plain text content
- Add table rows: use location=2/3 with table-row sectionId, format="html", markdown UNSUPPORTED
- Use analyzeStructure=true first to see available headings for documentRange
- Use returnSectionIds=true to get section IDs for precise targeting
- For adding content after headings like "Subsection 1.1", use location=6 with documentRange="Subsection 1.1"
- Prefer format="markdown" for most content

Markdown List Rules:
- Unordered lists MUST use * instead of - for list markers
- 4 spaces OR tab MUST be used to nest list items
- An additional newline MUST be between list label and its start
- REQUIRED extra newline between label and first list item
Example:
```
**Label:**

* Item one
    * Item one A
* Item two
```
Note: Prefer location=10 (AFTER_LIST_ITEM) or location=11 (BEFORE_LIST_ITEM) with sectionId from a list item for updates. These operations handle parent heading replacement for reliable nested list updates.

⚠️ CRITICAL WARNINGS:
- REPLACE_DOCUMENT_RANGE location=8 replaces the heading AND ALL CONTENT below until next heading of same level, ensure 'content' FULLY accounts for this
- Renaming ONLY a heading requires manually recreating the section structure
- Document ranges include subheadings: "Section 1" includes "Subsection 1.1", "Subsection 1.2", etc.
- Consider using AFTER_DOCUMENT_RANGE location=6 + DELETE_DOCUMENT_RANGE location=9 for complex restructuring

<Rule>ALWAYS use analyzeStructure=true first on a document to understand exact structure and observe what content will be affected</Rule>

format.description: Format of content. REQUIRED - must be explicitly specified, prefer 'markdown'

documentId.description: Quip document URL or ID. Examples: 'quip-amazon.com/ABC123/Doc' or 'ABC123'. OMIT for creation

sectionId.description: Section ID for targeted operations. Find in HTML IDs. REQUIRED for locations 2-5 (section operations) and 10-11 (list item operations)

returnSectionIds.description: Return section IDs for future targeted operations

mode.description: Link sharing mode: 'view' for view-only, 'edit' for edit access, 'none' to disable sharing. For new documents: configures sharing during creation. For existing documents: modifies current sharing settings

title.description: Title for new document. REQUIRED with 'content' parameter. OMIT to let Quip infer title from content

memberIds.description: Comma-separated folder/user IDs for document access. New documents only

type.description: Type of document to create. Default: 'document'

content.description: HTML or Markdown content to add/edit. Max 1MB. REQUIRED

contentFilePath.description: Local filepath to read content from. Takes precedence over 'content' field

documentRange.description: Heading text for document range operations. Must match exact heading text. Example: 'Subsection 1.1' or 'Topic 2 - Prerequisites'. REQUIRED for locations 6-9 - document range operations. Use analyzeStructure=true first to see headings

analyzeStructure.description: Parse and return document structure - headings, sections

ToolSpecification.name: TicketingWriteActions
ToolSpecification.description:
A tool for performing write operations on tickets in the ticketing system.
Provides confirmation of successful operations without requiring additional API calls.

Features:
1. Create new tickets with required CTI categorization
2. Update existing tickets with new information
3. Add comments to tickets with thread selection (CORRESPONDENCE, WORKLOG, ANNOUNCEMENTS)


## create-ticket
Create new tickets. **Cannot set severity to SEV_1, SEV_2.** Rate limited to 1 ticket per minute.

Parameters (title, description, severity, categorization required):
- title (REQUIRED): Ticket title
- description (REQUIRED): Ticket description  
- severity (REQUIRED): SEV_3, SEV_4, or SEV_5 only
- categorization (REQUIRED): CTI categorization array with at least 3 entries for category, type, and item
- assignedGroup, assignee, requester, hostname, estimatedStartTime, estimatedCompletionTime, needBy, tags, watchers (optional)

Example:
```json
{
  "action": "create-ticket",
  "title": "Server outage in production",
  "description": "Multiple users reporting connection timeouts",
  "severity": "SEV_3",
  "assignedGroup": "Infrastructure Team",
  "categorization": [
    { "key": "category", "value": "Infrastructure" },
    { "key": "type", "value": "Server" },
    { "key": "item", "value": "Connectivity" }
  ]
}
```

## update-ticket
Update existing tickets. **Cannot set severity to SEV_1, SEV_2, or SEV_2.5.**

Parameters (all optional except ticketId):
- ticketId (REQUIRED): Ticket ID to update
- title, description, status, severity, assignee, requester, categorization
- closureCode, resolution, rootCause, rootCauseDetails, pendingReason, hostname
- actualStartTime, actualCompletionTime, estimatedStartTime, estimatedCompletionTime, needBy (Unix timestamps)
- logTimeSpentInMinutes (can be positive/negative)
- tagsToAdd, tagsToRemove, watchersToAdd, watchersToRemove (arrays)

Returns: Success confirmation with ticket ID and operation status

Example:
```json
{
  "action": "update-ticket",
  "ticketId": "T123456",
  "status": "Resolved",
  "resolution": "Issue resolved by restarting the service"
}
```

## add-comment
Add a comment to an existing ticket.

Parameters:
- ticketId (REQUIRED): Ticket ID (e.g., T123456, V1679593024)
- message (REQUIRED): Comment text (3-60000 chars)
- threadName: "CORRESPONDENCE" (default), "WORKLOG", or "ANNOUNCEMENTS"
- contentType: "markdown" (default) or "plain"

Example:
```json
{
  "action": "add-comment",
  "ticketId": "T123456",
  "message": "Updated configuration and restarted service.",
  "threadName": "WORKLOG",
  "contentType": "plain"
}
```

⚠️ All parameters should be at the root level, not nested in an `input` object.

assignedGroup.description: Resolver group to assign ticket to (create-ticket only)

title.description: Ticket title (REQUIRED for create-ticket, optional for update-ticket)

ticketId.description: Ticket ID (REQUIRED for update-ticket, not used for create-ticket)

severity.description: Ticket severity (REQUIRED for create-ticket, optional for update-ticket, SEV_1 and SEV_2 blocked)

logTimeSpentInMinutes.description: Time spent update in minutes (positive or negative)

description.description: Ticket description (REQUIRED for create-ticket, optional for update-ticket)

ToolSpecification.name: MechanicDiscoverTools
ToolSpecification.description:
# Mechanic Tool Discovery Guide

## What is Mechanic
- Internal Amazon service providing CLI/web interface for operations
- Safer than AWS CLI with built-in guardrails and risk categorization
- Targets EC2 instances, Apollo hosts/hostclasses, ECS tasks
- Provides networking, logs, system information, and more

## Critical Discovery Rules
- ALWAYS verify tool exists in search results before suggesting
- NEVER assume tools exist based on naming conventions
- Show multiple options if unclear which tool helps user
- Use MechanicDescribeTool after discovery to get usage details
- If describe fails, tool doesn't exist - search again with different keywords

## Usage Best Practices
- Prefer batch operations with multiple values over separate commands
- Look for [Item1,Item2]... notation indicating multi-value support
- Chain multiple commands when single tool doesn't solve problem
- Ask about log limits when fetching logs if tool supports it
- If multiple tools are needed, discover them in the same command with multiple keywords

## Workflow Reference

# Mechanic Tools Workflow Guide

## Required 3-Step Process
1. DISCOVER → MechanicDiscoverTools (find tools)
2. DESCRIBE → MechanicDescribeTool (understand usage)
3. EXECUTE → MechanicRunTool (run with parameters)

## Critical Rule: Use MCP Tools Only
- ALWAYS use MechanicRunTool MCP tool
- NEVER execute mechanic CLI directly
- MCP provides validation, error handling, telemetry, and standardized output

## Step-by-Step Workflow

### 1. Discovery (MechanicDiscoverTools)
- Use relevant keywords to find appropriate tools
- Present multiple options if unsure
- If results don't match user needs: Explain and adjust keywords
- AWS resources: Search "aws" namespace first

### 2. Description (MechanicDescribeTool)
- Never skip this step - provides critical usage details
- Learn required/optional parameters and formats
- Always confirm with user that this is the correct tool

### 3. Execution (MechanicRunTool)
- Format parameters as string array
- Ask user for unknown required values
- Summarize what tool will do before executing
- Show errors to user for troubleshooting

## Common Patterns

### AWS Resource Operations
1. Discover listing tools ("ec2 list", "cloudwatch logs")
2. Execute listing tool to get resource IDs
3. Discover operation tools for those resources
4. Execute operation with obtained IDs

### Troubleshooting Sequence
1. General system information tools
2. Component-specific diagnostics
3. Detailed log analysis tools

## Best Practices
- Follow complete workflow for every operation
- Explain reasoning when searching for tools
- Break complex operations into multiple tool executions
- Return to discovery if tool doesn't solve problem
- Keep user informed at each step



# Workflow Examples

## Host Network Check
```
1. MechanicDiscoverTools(keywords=["network", "host"])
   → Found "host network route-table"
   
2. MechanicDescribeTool(namespace="host", toolPath="network route-table")
   → Requires --host parameter
   
3. MechanicRunTool(
     namespace="host", toolPath="network route-table",
     cluster="corp-pdx", args=["--host", "hostname.amazon.com"]
   )
```

## Host Patching
// involves patching yum packages, followed by a host reboot to apply updates
```
1. MechanicDiscoverTools(keywords=["patch", "update", "reboot"])
   → Found "host package update-security"

2. MechanicRunTool(
     namespace="host", toolPath="package update-security",
     cluster="corp-pdx", args=["--host", "hostname.amazon.com"]
   )
   → Returns user input request with request and execution id, ask user for input

3. MechanicSetUserInput(
     executionId="123", requestId="456", response="Yes"
   )
   → Returns output

4. MechanicRunTool(
     namespace="host", toolPath="system reboot",
     cluster="corp-pdx", args=["--host", "hostname.amazon.com"]
   )
   → Returns user input request like step 2
   // Command will error with ssh issue because the host is rebooting, after reboot patch will be applied

5. Same as step 3

```

## CloudWatch Log Analysis
```
1. MechanicDiscoverTools(keywords=["cloudwatch", "logs"])
   → Found "aws cloudwatch logs describe-log-groups"
   
2. MechanicRunTool(
     namespace="aws", toolPath="cloudwatch logs describe-log-groups",
     cluster="us-west-2", args=["--account", "123456789", "--role-name", "mechanic"]
   )
   → Returns log group "/aws/lambda/my-function"
   
3. MechanicDiscoverTools(keywords=["cloudwatch", "query"])
   → Found "aws cloudwatch logs query-logs"
   
4. MechanicRunTool(
     namespace="aws", toolPath="cloudwatch logs query-logs",
     cluster="us-west-2",
     args=[
       "--account", "123456789", "--role-name", "mechanic",
       "--log-group-name", "/aws/lambda/my-function",
       "--query", "fields @timestamp, @message | filter @message like /(?i)error/"
     ]
   )
```

oneOf.description:
# Keywords Parameter Guide

Format: JSON array of strings (NOT string representation)
- ✅ "keywords": ["network", "system", "route"]
- ❌ "keywords": "["network", "host", "route"]"

## Keyword Strategy
AVOID "host" or "aws" keywords unless absolutely necessary - they return too many tools.

PREFER specific namespace keywords:
- Host Namespace: system, network, file, disk, java, metric-agent, snitch, snape, time, odin, package, tps-generatordeployment, apollo
- AWS Namespace: cloudwatch, ec2, ecs, ssm, timber

Use sparingly (only when namespace keywords insufficient):
- Resource Types: host, hostclass, ec2, ecs

Important: Some namespaces have duplicate tools available in both host and aws namespaces. In these cases, prefer using the specific host or aws namespace tools rather than generic alternatives.

Notes: No keywords = all tools. Prefer namespace over resource type keywords for focused results.
Keywords as a JSON string of an array

ToolSpecification.name: GetPipelinesRelevantToUser
ToolSpecification.description:
Retrieves pipelines relevant to the current user or a specific user.
    
    This includes all pipelines the user has permissions on, including their favorites, and all pipelines grouped by team.
    
    The response includes:
    - Pipelines the user has marked as 'Favorite'
    - Pipelines the user has permissions on, grouped by team

user.description: Optional user alias to get pipelines for. If not provided, defaults to the current user

ToolSpecification.name: Delegate
ToolSpecification.description:
Orchestrates parallel and sequential execution of sub-tasks with dependency management:
• Readonly tasks run in parallel (batches of 10), write tasks sequentially
• Dependencies enforced via dependentIdentifiers with cycle detection
• Each delegate gets full tool access and conversation context
• Results from dependencies included in delegate prompts
• Configurable model selection per delegate
• Results maintain input ordering
When to use:
- Large token consuming files (images, xlsx, etc.)
- Multiple perspective analysis or explicitly requested sub-agents
- Sequential dependent tasks (example: unit test updates needing final summary)

prompt.description: The prompt to run. This will be passed to the LLM

modelArn.description:
Model: sonnet, haiku, opus, etc. or full ARN
Default anthropic.claude-3-5-haiku-20241022-v1:0 only set IF explicitly requested

readonly.description: Whether to use the model in read-only mode. This automatically allows for parallel execution for analysis tasks

parallel.description: Whether to use parallel mode. Disables custom tools, acts like readonly unless auto-accept-edits enabled for parallelized writes

items.description: The identifier of a delegate that must be completed before this prompt can be run. That prior delegate's response will be included

ToolSpecification.name: TaskeiUpdateTask
ToolSpecification.description: Update an existing Taskei task with new details. Taskei tasks are also known as SIM Issues, so this tool works for both Taskei and SIM

postCommentMessage.description: Comment to post in the task. Accepts markdown and plain text format

addLabels.description: Label UUIDs. Use TaskeiGetRoomResources to get available label IDs

rank.description: New rank for the task. -1 to clear

transferRoom.description: Transfers task to new room. UUID

archived.description: Whether to mark the task as archived

assignee.description: Username of the new assignee. Sending "currentUser" assigns the task to the user who performs the request

oneOf.description: String, Multiline Markdown or ISO-8601 datetime
ALWAYS use array format: single select = [one item], multi select = [multiple items]. Multi-select: include ALL options with selected: true/false (deselection needs to explicitly set to false)

items.description: Custom attribute - value type determined by ID prefix. No object types

ToolSpecification.name: GKAnalyzeVersionSet
ToolSpecification.description:
Analyzes a version set or Brazil workspace using the GordianKnot gk-analyze-version-set CLI tool.
This tool helps identify stale, unused packages and dependency conflicts in your Brazil version set. It provides recommendations for resolving issues
and improving the health of your dependency graph.

Common use cases:
1. Analyzing version set health:
  - Run analysis on an input version set or Brazil workspace to identify dependency issues
  - Get recommendations for resolving conflicts
  - Identify stale or unused packages

2. Troubleshooting dependency issues:
  - Diagnose build failures related to dependencies
  - Identify conflicting package versions
  - Find circular dependencies

For more information: tiny.amazon.com/wms0pm5v

additionalArgs.description: Optional additional arguments for the CLI, use --help for full list

workingDirectory.description: Optional working directory to get version set from. Supports relative or absolute path

versionSet.description: Optional input version set to analyze software health issues. If not provided, analyzes the current directory

ToolSpecification.name: SearchAcronymCentral
ToolSpecification.description:
Search Amazon's internal Acronym Central database

Returns acronym definitions with:
- Exact match search (case-insensitive)
- Full definitions with source URLs
- Associated tags for context and reliability

ToolSpecification.name: SimAddComment
ToolSpecification.description:
Add a plain text comment to an existing SIM issue given its ID or alias.
**Important**: This tool is only for SIM Classic. Prefer the following alternatives:
- For Tickets: Use the add-comment action as part of TicketingWriteActions
- For Taskei Tasks/Issues: Use TaskeiUpdateTask with the postCommentMessage parameter

issueId.description: Issue ID or alias (example P12345678 or CFN-12345)

comment.description: Comment text to add to the issue

ToolSpecification.name: GetSasCampaigns
ToolSpecification.description:
A tool for retrieving SAS campaigns from the Software Assurance Service (SAS).

Features:
1. get-user-campaigns: Get campaigns for specific user

Parameters:

get-user-campaigns: username: string OPTIONAL - Username to get campaigns for DEFAULT: current_user

action.description:
The action to perform.

Available actions:
1. get-user-campaigns: Get campaigns for specific user

Parameters:

get-user-campaigns: username: string OPTIONAL - Username to get campaigns for DEFAULT: current_user

ToolSpecification.name: MechanicSetUserInput
ToolSpecification.description:
This tool is for helping you send user input to a running Mechanic execution.
You provide the parameters to help identify the User Input request, and the response value, and this sends it to Mechanic and then continues executing the tool.
After running this tool, you will either get another user input request, or the execution will finish and output will be returned.

executionId.description: The ID for the execution to send user input to, do not make up this value. You MUST use a real execution ID

requestId.description: The ID for the user input request, do not make up this value. You MUST use a real user input request ID

response.description: User input response to the request. Must be "Yes" or "No"

ToolSpecification.name: CheckFilepathForCAZ
ToolSpecification.description: Checks if a filepath is protected by Contingent Authorization (CAZ), specifically whether it has customer data risk or security metadata risk.

hostclass.description: Optional Apollo hostclass name. If provided, AWS resource parameters are ignored

filepath.description: The file path to check for CAZ protection

account_id.description: AWS account ID for the resource

ToolSpecification.name: GetPipelineDetails
ToolSpecification.description:
Retrieves a detailed summary of a pipeline's current state, including:
    - Name, ID, description, enabled status
    - Health metrics including failed builds, deployments, tests, and pending approvals
    - Stage count by prod/non-prod and type
    - Target count by type and approval status
    - Promotion count by type and status
    - Latest events for targets in the pipeline
    - Active Administrative disables

    Definitions:
    - Badge indicates the automation level of the pipeline (gold: fully automated; silver: mostly automated; bronze: partially automated; no badge: not automated)
    - Promotions needing synchronization indicate a newer artifact is ready to be deployed to the next target in the pipeline

    This tool can retrieve information about any existing pipeline, not only those in the list of pipelines relevant to a user.

pipelineName.description: Name of the pipeline to get an overview summary for

ToolSpecification.name: CreatePackage
ToolSpecification.description:
Create Amazon software packages/repositories using BuilderHub templates for Python, Java, Javascript/Typescript, and other languages
Supports libraries, services, CLI tools, Lambda functions, etc following internal best practices

Actions:
• list - Show available templates for your dependency model (Brazil/Peru). Use when starting a new package
• create - Generate new package from template. Use after selecting template from list
• upload - Publish package to Gitfarm. Use after local development is complete

<Rule>Read packageInfo before list action unless dependency model known</Rule>
<Rule>List templates before create unless valid packageId known</Rule>
<Rule>Template dependency model must match workspace (brazil/peru)</Rule>
<Rule>Ask about upload after successful create</Rule>
<Rule>User must specify bindleId and clarify workingDirectory before upload action</Rule>
<Rule>Use absolute paths for workingDirectory</Rule>

packageId.description: Template ID from list action (required for create)

bindleId.description: Bindle ID for upload destination REQUIRED

workingDirectory.description: Absolute workspace path (required for create/upload, use pwd for current)

primaryExportControlType.description: Export control category (required for upload, see tiny.amazon.com/wq32lozq)

private.description: Mark package private in Bindles (optional for upload)

enableBranchProtection.description: Require CRUX UI for mainline changes (optional for upload)

containsEncryption.description: Has encryption/crypto functionality (required for HPC, IC, Nav, Telecom, none export types)

ToolSpecification.name: GetPolicyEngineDashboard
ToolSpecification.description: Gets the PolicyEngine risk dashboard for specified user.

userAlias.description: Alias of the risk owner whose dashboard is to be returned

ToolSpecification.name: GetDogmaClassification
ToolSpecification.description:
Fetch Dogma classification of a given pipeline
Dogma is a website that lets engineers and managers configure their Release Excellence rules. It allows the customer to: 
- View risks that apply to the pipelines they own
- Dive into details for each risk
- Request exemptions from rules that should not have reported a risk
- Manage pipeline classification and override values
- opt into new rules at the organization, team, or pipeline scope.
Dogma classification is a key feature in Dogma that automatically categorizes pipelines based on what is being deployed through them. This classification determines which policies and rules apply to each pipeline.
The classification structure includes:
- Inferred classification: Automatically determined by DogmaClassifier
- Classification overrides: Manual corrections to the inferred values when needed
- Custom classifications: Flexible key-value pairs for campaign targeting
The top-level fields represent the effective classification values that are currently active for the pipeline, taking into account both inferred data and any applied overrides.
More classification definition details are defined in the wiki: tiny.amazon.com/1e4sgmu23

ToolSpecification.name: BarristerEvaluationWorkflow
ToolSpecification.description:
If a user wants to perform a Barrister evaluation, this tool can be called.
    A Barrister evaluation is a risk evaluation check, to determine if a set of evidence (ex: SIMTT/2PR/MCM/IsProduction/ChangeControl/etc)
    is sufficient (compliant) in justifying an action. This is typically used for Contingent Authorization, but has applications in availabilty risk checks.
    Users should provide an initial namespace to evaluate against (example: amazon.barrister.v1).
    Follow the instructions for prompting the user in the "userInputDescription" return with every execution of this tool.

state.description: Current state of the tool (for state persistence)

stateData.description: State data for the current state (for state persistence)

context.description: The context being built for evaluation

selectedPolicyId.description: The ID of the selected policy

selectedConditions.description: The conditions IDs from the selected path to compliance in order to context build for

ToolSpecification.name: TicketingReadActions
ToolSpecification.description:
A tool for reading data from the ticketing system.

Features:
1. Search for tickets with various filters
2. Get the details of a single ticket
3. Get list of resolver groups user belongs to
4. Get details for a specific resolver group
5. Get comprehensive instructions for using the ticketing search functionality


# Ticketing Tools

These tools provide access to the ticketing system.

## How to Use

All actions require a JSON payload with the following structure:
```json
{
  "action": "<action-name>",
  "input": {
    // Action-specific parameters go here
  }
}
```

⚠️ Important: All parameters must be inside the `input` object. Parameters at the root level will not be processed correctly.

## Available Actions

### Ticket Search and Retrieval

#### search-tickets
Search for tickets based on various criteria.

Parameters:
- query: Raw Solr query string for custom searches. Example: 'extensions.tt.status:(Open OR "In Progress") AND extensions.tt.assignedGroup:"SWIM Front End"'
- status: Array of ticket statuses to filter by. By default, only open status tickets are returned.
- assignedGroup: Array of resolver group names to filter by. Example: ['MY TEAM', 'super-cool-team']
- fullText: Full text search term to search across ticket content. Example: 'error in production'
- createDate: Filter by creation date using Solr date syntax. Example: '[2024-01-01T00:00:00Z TO NOW]'
- lastResolvedDate: Filter by last resolved date using Solr date syntax.
- lastUpdatedDate: Filter by last updated date using Solr date syntax.
- currentSeverity: Array of severity levels to filter by. High severity is 1-2, 2.5 for business hours high severity, low severity is 3-5.
- minimumSeverity: A single number representing the minimum numeric ticket severity
- sort: Sort parameter for results. Example: 'lastUpdatedDate desc'
- rows: Maximum number of tickets to return (default: 50, max: 100)
- start: Starting index for pagination
- startToken: Token for cursor-based pagination
- responseFields: Array of fields to include in the response

For comprehensive search instructions and field descriptions, use the get-search-instructions action.

Example:
```
{
  "action": "search-tickets",
  "input": {
    "status": ["Assigned", "Researching", "Work In Progress", "Pending", "Resolved"],
    "assignedGroup": ["IT Support"],
    "minimumSeverity": 2,
    "createDate": "[2024-01-01T00:00:00Z TO NOW]",
    "sort": "lastUpdatedDate desc",
    "rows": 50,
    "responseFields": [
      "id",
      "title",
      "status",
      "extensions.tt.assignedGroup",
      "extensions.tt.impact",
      "createDate",
      "lastUpdatedDate",
      "description"
    ]
  }
}
```

Note: Some fields are nested under `extensions.tt` and must be referenced using dot notation (e.g., `extensions.tt.assignedGroup`). For a complete list of available fields, use the get-search-instructions action.

#### get-ticket
Retrieve a single ticket for a specified ID

Parameters:
- ticketId: The ID of the ticket

Response includes:
- Ticket details with the most recent announcement and 100 comments + worklogs

Example:
```json
{
  "action": "get-ticket",
  "input": {
    "ticketId": "ABC123"
  }
}
```

### Resolver Group Management

#### get-my-resolver-groups
Get the resolver groups that the current user is a member of.

Parameters: None

Example:
```
{
  "action": "get-my-resolver-groups"
}
```

#### get-resolver-group-details
Get operational details about a specific resolver group, including its configuration, members, and settings.

Parameters:
- groupName: The name of the resolver group to get details for

Response includes:
- Basic group information and group details
- Ownership information
- Business hours and days configuration
- Management structure, group preferences and settings
- Notification configurations
- Labels and templates

Example:
```json
{
  "action": "get-resolver-group-details",
  "input": {
    "groupName": "example-group"
  }
}
```

⚠️ Common Mistake: Do not put parameters at the root level. This will not work:
```json
{
  "action": "get-resolver-group-details",
  "groupName": "example-group"  // ❌ Wrong: parameter at root level
}
```

### Documentation and Instructions

#### get-search-instructions
Get comprehensive instructions for using the ticketing search functionality, including field descriptions, examples, and best practices.

Parameters: None

Example:
```
{
  "action": "get-search-instructions"
}
```

The response includes detailed information about:
- Available search fields and their properties
- Search syntax and examples
- Best practices for constructing queries

action.description:
The action to perform.

Available actions:
1. search-tickets: Search for tickets with various filters
2. get-ticket: Get the details of a single ticket
3. get-my-resolver-groups: Get list of resolver groups user belongs to
4. get-resolver-group-details: Get details for a specific resolver group
5. get-search-instructions: Get comprehensive instructions for using the ticketing search functionality

ToolSpecification.name: GetSasRisks
ToolSpecification.description:
A tool for reading sas risks from the Software Assurance Service (SAS).

Features:
1. get-user-risks: Get risks for specific user
2. get-user-risk-summary: Get summary of risks for specific user
3. get-brazil-version-set-risks: Get risks for specific Brazil version set
4. get-pipeline-risks: Get risks for pipeline (includes pipeline-target data) or pipeline-target only
5. get-apollo-risks: Get risks for specific Apollo resource
6. get-campaign-specific-risks: Get campaign specific risks

Parameters:

get-user-risks: username: string OPTIONAL - Username to get risks for DEFAULT: current_user
campaignType: string OPTIONAL - Campaign type DEFAULT: NON_ADVISORY
includeDetailedRisks: boolean OPTIONAL - Whether to include detailed risks in addition to summary DEFAULT: false

get-user-risk-summary: username: string OPTIONAL - Username to get risk summary for DEFAULT: current_user

get-brazil-version-set-risks: resourceName: string REQUIRED - Name of the Brazil VS

get-pipeline-risks: resourceName: string REQUIRED - Name of the pipeline or pipeline target
requestType: string OPTIONAL - 'pipeline' (default, includes pipeline-target data) or 'pipeline-target' (only pipeline-target data)

get-apollo-risks: resourceName: string REQUIRED - Name of the Apollo resource

get-campaign-specific-risks: username: string OPTIONAL - Username to get risks for DEFAULT: current_user
campaignId: string REQUIRED - Campaign ID for risk retrieval

ToolSpecification.name: CRRevisionCreator
ToolSpecification.description:
Creates a new code review revision from a workspace or updates an existing code review.
    A code review is a way to track proposed git changes to Amazon software packages.
    Code reviews can have multiple revisions.

    This does NOT create git commits. Git commits MUST be staged before using this tool.

    Common workflows this tool can be used in:
    1. Creating a new code review:
      - Files are modified in a package in a workspace.
      - A git commit (or commits) are staged locally.
      - This tool is used with the working directory and package names (without crId).
      - Result: a new code review revision is created for the commit(s) staged.
    
    2. Updating an existing code review:
      - A code review already exists with a known CR-ID (such as CR-221928836).
      - Files are modified in a package in a workspace.
      - This tool is used with the working directory, package names, and the existing crId.
      - Result: The existing code review is updated with a new revision for the staged commits.

workingDirectory.description: Working directory where a package lives that can be modified for a code review should be created

description.description: Optional description for the code review revision

packageNames.description: Array of packages names to include in the code review revision

items.description: The name of the package. This MUST exist in the workingDirectory

crId.description: Optional existing Code Review ID (such as CR-221928836). If provided, updates the existing CR instead of creating a new one

ToolSpecification.name: ApolloReadActions
ToolSpecification.description:
A tool for reading data from the Apollo deployment system.
Use for reading environment, stage, deployment, capacity, and configuration data.

Available actions and parameters:
- describe-environment: environmentName REQUIRED, includeInheritedProperties (optional boolean, default true)
- describe-environment-stage: environmentName REQUIRED, stage REQUIRED, includeInheritedProperties (optional boolean, default true)
- describe-deployment: deploymentId REQUIRED
- list-deployments-for-environment-stage: environmentName REQUIRED, stage REQUIRED, notBefore/notAfter (optional timestamps), fleetwide (optional boolean), packageChanging/composeInstructionChanging/queued/inProgress/finished (optional booleans, only use when explictly mentioned by user), maxResults/marker (optional numbers for pagination)
- describe-capacity: capacityName REQUIRED
- describe-environment-stage-capacity: environmentName REQUIRED, stage REQUIRED; use to get capacity for the environment stage
- describe-deployment-preference-set: deploymentPreferenceSetName REQUIRED; dps name can be obtained by describing environment stage
- describe-environment-op-config: environmentName REQUIRED, includeInheritedValues (optional boolean, default true)
- describe-environment-stage-op-config: environmentName REQUIRED, stage REQUIRED, includeInheritedValues (optional boolean, default true)
- list-environment-stages-by-name-substring: nameSubstring REQUIRED, marker (optional string), maxResults (optional number)
- list-audit-log-for-environment-and-stages: environmentName REQUIRED, startTime/endTime (optional timestamps); use to find any changes in environment / environment stage or any configuration

Example: { "action": "describe-environment", "environmentName": "my-environment" }

action.description: The Apollo action to perform. See documentation for details.

model_info.description: Models chosen by task for optimal usage and consistent quality
